title: The Language of Colour
summary: An exploration of how different languages and cultures categorise and perceive colour, and what this reveals about the relationship between language and thought.
body: Colour seems like an objective feature of the physical world — a property of light that exists independently of whoever is looking at it. Yet the way humans perceive, name, and think about colour turns out to be surprisingly shaped by the language they speak. This discovery has fascinated linguists and cognitive scientists for decades, and it continues to challenge our assumptions about the boundaries of perception. In English, the word 'blue' covers a broad spectrum — from the pale wash of a winter sky to the deep indigo of a night sea. But in Russian, there are two separate basic colour terms: 'goluboy' for light blue and 'siniy' for dark blue. Russian speakers do not just have different words for these shades; experiments have shown that they actually perceive the boundary between them more sharply than English speakers do. The categories provided by their language appear to quicken colour discrimination at the boundary. This phenomenon is known as categorical perception, and it suggests that language does not merely label a pre-existing reality but actively shapes how we experience it. The debate this raises is an old one in linguistics and philosophy: the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, or linguistic relativity, proposes that the structure of a language influences the way its speakers conceptualise the world. A strong version of this hypothesis — the idea that language determines thought entirely — has largely been discredited. But a weaker, more defensible version, which holds that language influences habitual patterns of thought, has found significant empirical support. Colour is one of the most compelling arenas for this debate because it involves a continuous physical spectrum that different languages carve up in different ways. The Pirahã people of the Amazon, for instance, have no fixed colour terms at all, describing colours only in relation to concrete objects — the red of blood, the green of a particular leaf. Some languages spoken in parts of Africa distinguish between shades that English-speaking scientists once assumed to be the same. The Himba people of Namibia have been studied extensively: they group what English speakers would call green and blue under one term, yet they distinguish several shades within what English speakers call green. When shown arrays of coloured tiles, Himba participants identified differences within green far more quickly than differences between green and blue, the reverse of what English speakers typically do. These findings carry broader implications. They remind us that the categories we use to organise experience are not universal givens but cultural constructions, passed on through language. They also raise an intriguing question: if learning a new language genuinely opens up new ways of perceiving the world, then multilingualism may offer cognitive benefits that go well beyond mere communication. Perhaps the language we speak is not just a tool for expressing what we already see, but a lens through which we see in the first place.

annotations.exampleEn: Scientists try to measure objective features of the world, not subjective impressions.

annotations.meaningVi: các nhà khoa học nhận thức
annotations.exampleEn: Cognitive scientists study how the brain processes information.
The term covers a broad spectrum of behaviours.
Categorical perception explains why we hear musical notes as distinct pitches.
Linguistic relativity is the theory that language shapes thought.
The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis sparked decades of debate in linguistics.

annotations.meaningVi: bị bác bỏ, mất uy tín
annotations.exampleEn: The theory was widely discredited after experiments failed to support it.
The hypothesis gained empirical support from laboratory studies.
Sport is one of the most compelling arenas for studying teamwork.
Different cultures carve up the colour spectrum in different ways.
Children often learn abstract concepts through concrete objects first.
Participants were shown arrays of images and asked to identify patterns.
Gender roles are often described as cultural constructions rather than biological facts.

annotations.meaningVi: những điều đương nhiên phổ quát
annotations.exampleEn: We should not treat our own cultural norms as universal givens.
Multilingualism is the norm in many parts of the world.
Learning a second language has cognitive benefits for older adults.
The test measured participants' colour discrimination abilities.

annotations.meaningVi: câu hỏi hấp dẫn, thú vị
annotations.exampleEn: The study raises an intriguing question about how memory works.
Culture is the lens through which we interpret social behaviour.

annotations.meaningVi: các khuôn mẫu tư duy thói quen
annotations.exampleEn: Language may shape habitual patterns of thought without restricting them entirely.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice in Academic Description
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn viết học thuật và khoa học, câu bị động thường được sử dụng để mô tả các quy trình và kết quả mà không cần nhắc đến tác nhân cụ thể. Ví dụ: 'The Himba people have been studied extensively' (thay vì 'Researchers have studied the Himba people extensively') và 'has largely been discredited' (thay vì 'linguists have largely discredited it'). Cách dùng này tạo ra giọng điệu khách quan và trang trọng, phù hợp với văn phong học thuật.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In academic and scientific writing, passive voice is used to describe processes and findings without naming a specific agent. Examples: 'The Himba people have been studied extensively' and 'has largely been discredited'. Using passive shifts focus from who did the studying or discrediting to what was studied or discredited — creating the objective, impersonal tone that characterises academic prose.

grammarNotes.title: Relative Clauses with Quantifiers
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Mệnh đề quan hệ với 'which' hoặc 'that' kết hợp với lượng từ giúp làm rõ nghĩa cho danh từ đứng trước. Ví dụ: 'a continuous physical spectrum that different languages carve up in different ways' — mệnh đề 'that different languages carve up in different ways' bổ nghĩa cho 'spectrum'. Lưu ý rằng 'which' có thể thay thế 'that' trong mệnh đề không hạn định (non-restrictive), nhưng 'that' thường được dùng trong mệnh đề hạn định (restrictive) như trong văn học thuật.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Relative clauses introduced by 'which' or 'that' are used with quantifiers to specify a noun more precisely. In 'a continuous physical spectrum that different languages carve up in different ways', the relative clause restricts and defines 'spectrum'. 'That' is standard in restrictive relative clauses (essential to meaning), while 'which' (set off by commas) is used in non-restrictive ones (additional information). At B2 level, using these correctly greatly improves precision.

translationVi: Màu sắc có vẻ như là một đặc điểm khách quan của thế giới vật chất — một thuộc tính của ánh sáng tồn tại độc lập với bất kỳ ai nhìn vào nó. Tuy nhiên, cách con người nhận thức, đặt tên và suy nghĩ về màu sắc hóa ra lại bị định hình một cách đáng ngạc nhiên bởi ngôn ngữ họ nói. Khám phá này đã mê hoặc các nhà ngôn ngữ học và khoa học nhận thức trong nhiều thập kỷ, và nó tiếp tục thách thức các giả định của chúng ta về ranh giới của nhận thức. Trong tiếng Anh, từ 'blue' bao phủ một phổ rộng — từ màu xanh nhạt của bầu trời mùa đông đến màu chàm đậm của biển đêm. Nhưng trong tiếng Nga, có hai thuật ngữ màu cơ bản riêng biệt: 'goluboy' cho màu xanh nhạt và 'siniy' cho màu xanh đậm. Người nói tiếng Nga không chỉ có các từ khác nhau cho các sắc thái này; các thí nghiệm đã chỉ ra rằng họ thực sự nhận thức ranh giới giữa chúng sắc nét hơn so với người nói tiếng Anh. Các danh mục được cung cấp bởi ngôn ngữ của họ dường như tăng tốc độ phân biệt màu sắc tại ranh giới. Hiện tượng này được gọi là tri giác phân loại, và nó gợi ý rằng ngôn ngữ không chỉ đơn thuần gán nhãn cho một thực tế đã có trước mà còn tích cực định hình cách chúng ta trải nghiệm nó. Cuộc tranh luận mà điều này nêu ra là một cuộc tranh luận cũ trong ngôn ngữ học và triết học: giả thuyết Sapir-Whorf, hay thuyết tương đối ngôn ngữ, đề xuất rằng cấu trúc của ngôn ngữ ảnh hưởng đến cách người nói của nó nhận thức thế giới. Phiên bản mạnh của giả thuyết này — ý tưởng rằng ngôn ngữ hoàn toàn quyết định tư duy — phần lớn đã bị bác bỏ. Nhưng một phiên bản yếu hơn, có thể bảo vệ hơn, cho rằng ngôn ngữ ảnh hưởng đến các khuôn mẫu tư duy thói quen, đã tìm được sự ủng hộ thực nghiệm đáng kể. Màu sắc là một trong những lĩnh vực hấp dẫn nhất cho cuộc tranh luận này vì nó liên quan đến một phổ vật lý liên tục mà các ngôn ngữ khác nhau phân chia theo những cách khác nhau. Người Pirahã ở Amazon, chẳng hạn, hoàn toàn không có các thuật ngữ màu cố định, mô tả màu sắc chỉ liên quan đến các vật thể cụ thể — màu đỏ của máu, màu xanh lá của một loại lá cụ thể. Một số ngôn ngữ được nói ở một số vùng ở châu Phi phân biệt giữa các sắc thái mà các nhà khoa học nói tiếng Anh từng cho là giống nhau. Người Himba ở Namibia đã được nghiên cứu rộng rãi: họ nhóm những gì người nói tiếng Anh gọi là xanh lá và xanh dương dưới một thuật ngữ, nhưng họ phân biệt nhiều sắc thái trong những gì người nói tiếng Anh gọi là màu xanh lá. Khi được cho xem các mảng gạch màu, những người tham gia Himba xác định sự khác biệt trong màu xanh lá nhanh hơn nhiều so với sự khác biệt giữa xanh lá và xanh dương, ngược lại với những gì người nói tiếng Anh thường làm. Những phát hiện này có ý nghĩa rộng hơn. Chúng nhắc nhở chúng ta rằng các danh mục chúng ta sử dụng để tổ chức trải nghiệm không phải là những điều đương nhiên phổ quát mà là các kiến tạo văn hóa, được truyền đạt qua ngôn ngữ. Chúng cũng đặt ra một câu hỏi hấp dẫn: nếu việc học một ngôn ngữ mới thực sự mở ra những cách mới để nhận thức thế giới, thì đa ngôn ngữ có thể mang lại những lợi ích nhận thức vượt xa giao tiếp đơn thuần. Có lẽ ngôn ngữ chúng ta nói không chỉ là một công cụ để diễn đạt những gì chúng ta đã thấy, mà còn là lăng kính mà qua đó chúng ta nhìn ngay từ đầu.

questions.prompt: What is the central claim of the passage about colour and language?
options: Colour is entirely determined by physics and has nothing to do with language
How we perceive colour is surprisingly shaped by the language we speak
All languages perceive colour in exactly the same way
Colour terms are universal across all human languages
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'the way humans perceive, name, and think about colour turns out to be surprisingly shaped by the language they speak'.
questions.hint: Look for the main thesis in the opening paragraph.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between how English and Russian treat the colour blue?
options: English has more words for blue than Russian
Russian treats all shades of blue as one category
Russian has two basic terms for blue, while English has one
English speakers perceive blue more precisely than Russian speakers
questions.explanation: Russian has 'goluboy' for light blue and 'siniy' for dark blue, while English uses one word, 'blue', for both.
questions.hint: The passage compares English and Russian colour vocabulary specifically.

questions.prompt: What did experiments show about Russian speakers and the boundary between light and dark blue?
options: They were slower to distinguish the shades than English speakers
They perceived the boundary between the shades more sharply than English speakers
They considered the two shades to be identical
They performed identically to English speakers
questions.explanation: Experiments showed that Russian speakers 'actually perceive the boundary between them more sharply than English speakers do'.
questions.hint: Think about what having two different words for blue does to perception.

questions.prompt: What is the term for the phenomenon where language categories quicken colour discrimination?
questions.explanation: The passage describes this as 'categorical perception'.
questions.hint: The term is introduced after the Russian blue example.

questions.prompt: What does the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis propose?
options: That language and thought are completely separate systems
That colour perception is genetically determined
That the structure of a language influences the way its speakers conceptualise the world
That all languages evolved from a single ancient language
questions.explanation: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis 'proposes that the structure of a language influences the way its speakers conceptualise the world'.
questions.hint: The hypothesis is also called 'linguistic relativity'.

questions.prompt: Which version of the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis has been largely discredited?
options: The weak version, which says language influences thought
The version related to colour perception specifically
The strong version, which says language determines thought entirely
The version supported by the Himba studies
questions.explanation: The 'strong version — the idea that language determines thought entirely — has largely been discredited'.
questions.hint: The passage distinguishes a strong and a weaker version of the hypothesis.

questions.prompt: How do the Pirahã people describe colours?
options: Using a complex system of twelve basic terms
Only in relation to concrete objects
With a single term for all warm colours
They have no way of describing colour at all
questions.explanation: The Pirahã 'describe colours only in relation to concrete objects — the red of blood, the green of a particular leaf'.
questions.hint: The Pirahã have no fixed colour terms.

questions.prompt: What is unusual about how the Himba people of Namibia categorise colour?
options: They use the same term for green and blue but distinguish multiple shades of green
They have no words for warm colours such as red and orange
They use the same term for green and yellow
They perceive colour in black and white due to a genetic trait
questions.explanation: The Himba 'group what English speakers would call green and blue under one term, yet distinguish several shades within what English speakers call green'.
questions.hint: Think about how Himba colour categories differ from English ones.

questions.prompt: How did Himba participants perform compared to English speakers in colour identification tasks?
options: They identified green-blue differences faster than English speakers
They identified differences within green faster, and green-blue differences more slowly than English speakers
Their performance was identical to English speakers
They identified all colour differences more slowly than English speakers
questions.explanation: Himba participants identified differences within green 'far more quickly than differences between green and blue, the reverse of what English speakers typically do'.
questions.hint: The comparison is described as 'the reverse of' what English speakers do.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what are the categories we use to organise experience?
options: Universal, biologically determined features of human cognition
Cultural constructions passed on through language
Random variations with no systematic basis
Fixed properties determined by geography and climate
questions.explanation: The passage states these categories 'are not universal givens but cultural constructions, passed on through language'.
questions.hint: Look for the broader implication the passage draws from the Himba findings.

questions.prompt: What cognitive benefit does the passage suggest multilingualism might offer?
options: Improved memory for numbers and sequences
The ability to learn languages more quickly over time
New ways of perceiving the world beyond mere communication
Greater accuracy in describing physical colours
questions.explanation: The passage suggests multilingualism 'may offer cognitive benefits that go well beyond mere communication'.
questions.hint: The benefit is linked to how language shapes perception.

questions.prompt: What metaphor does the passage use in its closing sentence to describe language's role?
options: A map that shows what already exists
A mirror that reflects what we think
A tool for shaping the physical world
A lens through which we see in the first place
questions.explanation: The passage closes with the metaphor of language as 'a lens through which we see in the first place'.
questions.hint: The metaphor involves an optical instrument.

questions.prompt: What does the passage say colour perception is particularly useful for exploring in linguistics?
options: The Sapir-Whorf debate, because colour involves a continuous spectrum carved up differently by different languages
The relationship between writing systems and speech
questions.explanation: Colour is 'one of the most compelling arenas for this debate because it involves a continuous physical spectrum that different languages carve up in different ways'.
questions.hint: The passage explains why colour is especially useful for the debate.

questions.prompt: What does the passage suggest about the relationship between language and reality?
options: Language merely labels a pre-existing reality without changing it
Language has no relationship with the physical world
Language actively shapes how we experience reality, not just how we label it
Reality is entirely constructed by language with no independent existence
questions.explanation: The passage argues that 'language does not merely label a pre-existing reality but actively shapes how we experience it'.
questions.hint: Look for the contrast between 'labelling' and 'shaping'.

questions.prompt: What field of science, alongside linguistics, is said to have been fascinated by the colour-language connection?
questions.explanation: The passage says the discovery 'has fascinated linguists and cognitive scientists for decades'.
questions.hint: Two fields are mentioned together in the opening paragraph.

cloze.template: The way humans perceive and name colour is shaped by the {{b1}} they speak. In Russian, there are two {{b2}} terms for blue, whereas English uses only one. Experiments show that Russian speakers perceive the {{b3}} between light and dark blue more sharply. This is known as {{b4}}. The {{b5}}, also called linguistic relativity, proposes that language influences how speakers conceptualise the world. The strong version of this hypothesis has been largely {{b6}}, but the weaker version has found {{b7}} support. The Pirahã people describe colours in relation to {{b8}} rather than using fixed colour terms. The Himba of Namibia group green and blue under {{b9}} term but distinguish several {{b10}} of green. Their colour categories are the {{b11}} of English speakers' categories. The passage argues that the categories we use to organise experience are not {{b12}} but cultural constructions passed on through language. {{b13}} may offer cognitive benefits beyond communication. Language may be not just a tool for expression, but a {{b14}} through which we see the world. The Sapir-Whorf debate is particularly compelling in the domain of colour because colour involves a {{b15}} physical spectrum.

blanks.explanation: Colour perception is shaped by 'the language they speak'.
Russian has 'two separate basic colour terms' for blue.
Russian speakers perceive 'the boundary between them more sharply'.
The theory is called 'the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, or linguistic relativity'.
The strong version 'has largely been discredited'.
The weaker version has found 'significant empirical support'.
The Pirahã describe colours 'in relation to concrete objects'.
The Himba 'group green and blue under one term'.
Himba people 'distinguish several shades within what English speakers call green'.
Himba colour perception is 'the reverse of what English speakers typically do'.
Categories 'are not universal givens but cultural constructions'.
'Multilingualism may offer cognitive benefits that go well beyond mere communication'.
The closing metaphor describes language as 'a lens through which we see'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The passage suggests that the language we speak may function as a 'lens through which we see in the first place'. If this is true, does it mean that speakers of languages with fewer colour terms are at a perceptual disadvantage, or simply that they perceive the world differently? And what are the implications of this for how we think about the relationship between linguistic diversity and cognitive diversity?
